<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = Daisen8.JPG | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|23|18.9|N|133|32|19.4|E|region:JP-31_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www2.sanmedia.or.jp/oogamiyama/ Ōgamiyama Jinja homepage] * [http://www.daisen.jp/daisenji Official Home Page of Daisen Tourist Bureau]

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[:en:Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[:en:Daisen-ji|Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in [[:en:Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[:en:Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[:en:Japan|Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[:en:Important Cultural Property (Japan)|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[:en:Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[:en:Daisen-ji|Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = Daisen8.JPG | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|23|18.9|N|133|32|19.4|E|region:JP-31_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = File:Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.388583333333|N|133.53872222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Daisen|WD=Q1204975}}; {{ill|Yonago|WD=Q320964}} | part_of = {{ill|Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage|WD=Q11395860}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = Daisen8.JPG | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|23|18.9|N|133|32|19.4|E|region:JP-31_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www2.sanmedia.or.jp/oogamiyama/ Ōgamiyama Jinja homepage] * [http://www.daisen.jp/daisenji Official Home Page of Daisen Tourist Bureau]

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = Daisen8.JPG | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|23|18.9|N|133|32|19.4|E|region:JP-31_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www2.sanmedia.or.jp/oogamiyama/ Ōgamiyama Jinja homepage] * [http://www.daisen.jp/daisenji Official Home Page of Daisen Tourist Bureau]

A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto|1=Kishimoto, Tottori}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji|1=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = File:Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.388583333333|N|133.53872222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Daisen|WD=Q1204975|1=Daisen, Tottori}}; {{ill|Yonago|WD=Q320964|1=Yonago}} | part_of = {{ill|Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage|WD=Q11395860|1=Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen|1=Daisen, Tottori}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto|1=Kishimoto, Tottori}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji|1=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ogamiyama-jinja (Tottori)]][[en:Ōgamiyama Shrine]][[fr:Ōgamiyama-jinja]][[ja:大神山神社]][[pt:Santuário Ōgamiyama]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = Daisen8.JPG | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|23|18.9|N|133|32|19.4|E|region:JP-31_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Ōkuninushi]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
==References== {{Reflist}}
==External links== * {{in lang|ja}} [http://www2.sanmedia.or.jp/oogamiyama/ Ōgamiyama Jinja homepage] * [http://www.daisen.jp/daisenji Official Home Page of Daisen Tourist Bureau]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = File:Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.388583333333|N|133.53872222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Daisen|WD=Q1204975|1=Daisen, Tottori}}; {{ill|Yonago|WD=Q320964|1=Yonago}} | part_of = {{ill|Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage|WD=Q11395860|1=Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen|1=Daisen, Tottori}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto|1=Kishimoto, Tottori}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji|1=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[Shrines|shrine]], in [[Daisen, Tottori|Daisen]], [[Tottori Prefecture|Tottori]], [[Japan]]. A number of its structures have been designated [[Important Cultural Properties of Japan|Important Cultural Properties]].
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from [[Daisen-ji]], and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Jinja and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>

The small building was set up as a shrine in the [[Heian period]].
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, [[Kishimoto, Tottori|Kishimoto]], but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.

The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Tottori Prefecture, Japan}} {{Use dmy dates|date=November 2019}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} | image = File:Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg | caption = The Main Building | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{Coord|35.388583333333|N|133.53872222222|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = | location = {{ill|Daisen|WD=Q1204975|1=Daisen, Tottori}}; {{ill|Yonago|WD=Q320964|1=Yonago}} | part_of = {{ill|Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage|WD=Q11395860|1=Izumo Shinbutsu Pilgrimage}} }}
{{nihongo|'''Ōgamiyama Jinja'''|大神山神社 |Ōgamiyama Jinja}} is a [[Shinto]] [[:en:Shrines|shrine]], in {{ill|Daisen, Tottori|en|Daisen, Tottori|WD=Q1204975|lt=Daisen|1=Daisen, Tottori}}, {{ill|Tottori Prefecture|en|Tottori Prefecture|WD=Q133935|lt=Tottori|1=Tottori Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. A number of its structures have been designated {{ill|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|en|Important Cultural Property (Japan)|WD=Q1188622|lt=Important Cultural Properties|1=Important Cultural Property (Japan)}}.
==Origins== Ōgamiyama Jinja is a complex of Shinto shrines, which were created to worship [[:en:Daisen (mountain)|Daisen]]. Daisen has been worshiped as a holy mountain at least since the 8th century. Originally this shrine was constructed as a simple building {{convert|998|m|ft}} above sea level on a peak {{convert|1725|m|ft}} in height. The small building was set up as a shrine in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.
==History== The original location of this shrine is covered by heavy snow in winter, so another branch of this shrine was built at the foot of the mountain. It is called the ''winter shrine''. In turn, the original shrine was called the ''summer shrine''. The winter shrine was originally built at Maruyama, {{ill|Kishimoto, Tottori|en|Kishimoto, Tottori|WD=Q6416593|lt=Kishimoto|1=Kishimoto, Tottori}}, but moved to Fukumahara and Odaka in [[:en:Yonago, Tottori|Yonago]]. The summer shrine has remained in its original location since Heian Period. With the [[Shinbutsu bunri]], literally Shinto-Buddhism-separation after the Meiji restoration, this shrine was formally separated from {{ill|Daisen-ji|en|Daisen-ji|WD=Q3012163|lt=Daisen-ji|1=Daisen-ji}}, and the summer shrine was renamed ''Okumiya'' of Ogami Shrine (disambiguation) and the Buddhist objects of worship were removed. The current main object of worship is "Oanamuchi no Kami".
In the [[modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]], Ōgamiyama was listed among the 3rd class of nationally significant shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kokuhei-shōsha]]''|国幣小社}}.
==Important Cultural Properties== * Okumiya [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2949 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * [[Setsumatsusha|Massha]] Shimoyama [[Honden]] - [[heiden (Shinto)|Heiden]] - [[haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]] (1805)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/102/2950 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref> * Large stone [[Torii]] (1854)<ref>{{cite web |url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/101/00002812 |title=Database of Registered National Cultural Properties |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=28 April 2011}}</ref>
